name: El Dorado
description: El Dorado, meaning "the gilded one" in Spanish, represents a legendary kingdom or city of immense wealth. Initially, it referred to a chieftain of the Muisca people near modern-day Colombia, who, as part of an initiation ritual, would cover himself in gold dust and plunge into Lake Guatavita. This act sparked tales of unimaginable riches hidden within the region. Over time, El Dorado transformed from a person to a place, a shimmering city built entirely of gold, hidden deep within the uncharted territories of South America. European explorers, driven by greed and the promise of untold fortunes, relentlessly searched for El Dorado for centuries, mapping vast regions and encountering diverse indigenous cultures. The myth fueled exploration and colonization, leaving a lasting impact on the history and culture of South America. Despite countless expeditions and the lack of any concrete evidence, the allure of El Dorado continues to captivate the imagination, symbolizing the eternal human desire for wealth, adventure, and the pursuit of the unattainable.
subtitle: The legendary city of gold.
location: Unspecified region of South America, often associated with Colombia, Venezuela, and Guyana.
significance: Represents the allure of wealth, the dangers of greed, and the impact of European colonization on indigenous populations.

keyMyths.title: The Gilded Man of Guatavita
keyMyths.description: The original El Dorado was a Muisca chieftain who, during his coronation, would cover himself in gold dust and jump into Lake Guatavita as an offering to the gods. This ritual became the foundation for the legend of a land overflowing with gold.

keyMyths.title: Orellana's Search for Cinnamon and Gold
keyMyths.description: Francisco de Orellana, searching for cinnamon, stumbled upon the Amazon River. He heard tales of a wealthy kingdom further inland, fueled by indigenous stories, leading him to believe he was close to discovering El Dorado, though he never found it.
keyMyths.source: Gaspar de Carvajal, Relación del nuevo descubrimiento del famoso río Grande que descubrió por muy grande el capitán Francisco de Orellana (1542)

keyMyths.title: Raleigh's Guiana Expedition
keyMyths.description: Sir Walter Raleigh, convinced of El Dorado's existence, led expeditions to Guiana (present-day Guyana) in search of the city. He wrote extensively about his supposed encounters with indigenous peoples who confirmed its existence, though his claims were largely fabricated.
keyMyths.source: Sir Walter Raleigh, The Discovery of Guiana (1596)

sources.author: Hemming, John
sources.description: A comprehensive historical account of the various expeditions and myths surrounding El Dorado.
sources.text: A comprehensive historical account of the various expeditions and myths surrounding El Dorado.

sources.author: Whitehead, Neil L.
sources.work: Darkness and the Glitter: Archaeology, Colonialism, and Racial Identity in the Guianas
sources.description: Examines the impact of the El Dorado myth on the indigenous populations of the Guianas and the construction of colonial identities.
sources.text: Examines the impact of the El Dorado myth on the indigenous populations of the Guianas and the construction of colonial identities.

sources.author: Silverblatt, Irene
sources.work: Moon, Sun, and Witches: Gender Ideologies and Class in Inca and Colonial Peru
sources.description: Provides context for the cultural significance of gold in pre-Columbian societies and its role in shaping the El Dorado myth.
sources.text: Provides context for the cultural significance of gold in pre-Columbian societies and its role in shaping the El Dorado myth.
